video_title: How Much Is $1 Trillion? | Animal Spirits 469
gist: Ben Carlson and Michael Batnick argue the AI cycle most likely ends in an 'unsatisfying' 25-30% washout and recovery rather than a dot-com-style crash, dissect the SpaceX IPO as small-float 'explainable lunacy' that briefly rivaled Amazon's market cap, and make the case that markets and the average investor are getting structurally smarter (oil, inflation, geopolitics shrugged off faster). Despite the title's $1-trillion framing, the bulk of the episode is a bull-market resilience thesis spanning AI dispersion, EM earnings, consumer strength from locked-in 3% mortgages, and AI's labor-market impact.

insights.claim: Carlson's base case for the AI cycle is an 'unsatisfying conclusion': a 25-30% washout/bear market that proves a buying opportunity rather than a dot-com-style life-altering crash, because AI is a genuine life-changing technology and the world moves on.
insights.speaker: Ben Carlson
insights.so_what: Implies positioning to buy a future 25-30% AI-led drawdown rather than trying to exit ahead of a terminal crash.
insights.quote: we get a wash out at some point, but it's not an end of the world crash and we and the world moves on because AI is a life-changing technology and that's probably a buying opportunity

insights.claim: The hosts argue investors do not need to 'call the top' on AI, noting permabears like Jim Grant, Ray Dalio and Jim Chanos have a livelihood incentive to call tops and that tech-bubble headlines have recurred since at least 2015-2017.
insights.speaker: Ben Carlson
insights.so_what: Discount top-calling pundits; staying invested through recurring bubble warnings has historically beaten timing the exit.
insights.quote: if anyone who does quote unquote call it is going to be lucky because they timed it right and they've been calling it for 12 years

insights.claim: Carlson contends the classic 'shoe-shine boy' top indicator is now obsolete because information is ubiquitous and there are 'too many anecdotes,' so a single retail anecdote (e.g. a man getting 75 SpaceX shares) no longer signals a market top.
insights.speaker: Ben Carlson
insights.so_what: Traditional sentiment 'top' signals are degraded; investors should weight them less than in prior cycles.
insights.quote: There are no more shoe shine boy indicators now

insights.claim: Batnick frames a future multi-year (potentially four-year) bear market as a near-certainty rather than a prediction, noting the U.S. has gone an unusually long stretch without a recession or financial crisis.
insights.speaker: Michael Batnick
insights.so_what: Sets expectation that a severe multi-year drawdown is overdue, supporting long-horizon risk budgeting even amid a strong bull market.
insights.quote: at some point, and this is not a prediction or a hot take or whatever, we will have a four-year bare market

insights.claim: Carlson predicts that if an AI-driven blowup occurs it will be extremely fast — roughly a 20-25% decline in about three weeks — because faster-digesting modern markets 'rip the band-aid off.'
insights.speaker: Ben Carlson
insights.so_what: An AI shock may be too fast to trade reactively; pre-set risk rules matter more than reacting after the fact.
insights.quote: if there is like an AI whatever it's gonna be like down 20 25% in like three weeks

themes.theme: SpaceX IPO mechanics and small-float dynamics

insights.claim: The SpaceX IPO traded roughly $85 billion in volume on Friday (per Eric Balchunas), a record for an IPO and a top-10 single-day figure for any stock, with the company approaching Amazon's market cap within days.
insights.speaker: Michael Batnick
insights.so_what: Signals extreme retail demand and a thin tradable float; the headline market cap is not a clean read on fundamental value.
insights.quote: It ended up doing $85 billion in volume according to Eric Bachunis. Easily a record for an IPO and in the top 10 all time for any stock on any day.

insights.claim: Batnick attributes SpaceX's surge to small-float mechanics — buyers are chasing a 20%-in-24-hours move rather than valuing it versus Amazon — which he calls 'explainable lunacy' and warns the price can be pushed around easily.
insights.speaker: Michael Batnick
insights.so_what: Treat the SpaceX print as flow-driven, not valuation-driven; the small float makes both spikes and reversals violent.
insights.quote: There are just not a lot of shares out there. And so, nobody who's buying today for the most part is thinking about is is SpaceX worth more than Amazon

insights.claim: Levered single-stock SpaceX ETFs are seeing record flows — the levered long traded $282 million and the levered short $219 million in one day — with the complex on track to gather roughly $5 billion quickly.
insights.speaker: Michael Batnick
insights.so_what: Heavy levered-ETF activity adds mechanical, gamma-like pressure that can amplify SpaceX volatility in both directions.
insights.quote: the levered long traded $282 million worth of shares and the leverage short, I don't know who is doing this, $219 million worth of shares

insights.claim: The hosts note SpaceX's IPO terms include escalation triggers that let insiders unlock and sell more shares as the price rises, which they say is fueling 'gaming the system' criticism even though the rules were disclosed.
insights.speaker: Michael Batnick
insights.so_what: Price-triggered unlocks create a structural supply overhang that could cap upside and pressure the stock back toward IPO levels.
insights.quote: There are escalations and triggers about if the shares are up x% then they are allowed to unlock more shares and sell into it

insights.claim: ARK Invest bought SpaceX shares at the IPO (not via a private allocation), holding about $325 million worth in ARKK as its largest position.
insights.speaker: Michael Batnick
insights.so_what: ARKK's largest position is now a thin-float, momentum-driven name, concentrating fund risk in SpaceX's volatility.
insights.quote: Arc SpaceX is the she owns $325 million worth of SpaceX in in ARKK alone

themes.theme: Dispersion, value vs growth, and the AI-vs-software split

insights.claim: Bank of America's Savita Subramanian warned it is time to take profits, citing that the spread (dispersion) between top and bottom tech performers now rivals the dot-com bubble.
insights.speaker: Ben Carlson
insights.so_what: A respected strategist's dispersion signal is a watch-item, but the hosts argue it mainly reflects an AI-vs-software split, not a broad top.
insights.quote: she said the spread between the top performers and the bottom performers in tech rivals the.com bubble

insights.claim: Batnick reframes the alarming tech-dispersion chart as simply AI versus software — the top quintile is AI/semis (e.g. Micron) and the bottom quintile is software (e.g. Salesforce) — making the dispersion less ominous than headlined.
insights.speaker: Michael Batnick
insights.so_what: Read tech dispersion as a sector rotation (AI winners vs software losers), not a bubble-top tell; software is the funding source.
insights.quote: What this is showing is AI versus software. That's all this is showing.

insights.claim: Value is beating growth in 2026, with Vanguard Value (VTV) outperforming Vanguard Growth (VUG) by roughly seven to eight percent year-to-date, partly because cheap memory/semi names now screen as value.
insights.speaker: Ben Carlson
insights.so_what: Value indices are capturing AI upside through cheap semis (Micron ~4% of VTV); the value/growth label is blurring.
insights.quote: Vanguard Value, which is VTV, is beating Vanguard Growth VUG by I think seven or eight% this year

insights.claim: The hosts argue the leadership story is broadening, noting the S&P 493 is outperforming the Magnificent 7 'by a lot,' which they view as healthy bull-market behavior rather than a warning.
insights.speaker: Michael Batnick
insights.so_what: Broadening leadership beyond hyperscalers supports staying long equities rather than fearing concentration unwind.
insights.quote: the S&P 493 are outperforming the Max 7 by a lot

insights.claim: Oil fell back to about $80 a barrel on another Iran deal despite analysts arguing a closed Strait of Hormuz should have pushed prices to $150-200, which Carlson cites as proof markets correctly looked through the geopolitical scare.
insights.speaker: Ben Carlson
insights.so_what: Markets increasingly fade geopolitical supply scares; fading oil spikes on Middle East headlines has been a repeatable edge this decade.
insights.quote: the energy analysts were ripping their hair out for weeks saying the straight of hormuz is closed... It never got close to there

insights.claim: Carlson's thesis is that markets are becoming structurally smarter — 'their own form of AI' — because a smarter average investor digests headline shocks faster, reducing panic and shortening selloffs.
insights.speaker: Ben Carlson
insights.so_what: If shocks are digested faster, drawdowns are shorter and sharper, favoring buy-the-dip over waiting for prolonged capitulation.
insights.quote: markets are now markets are like their own form of AI. The intelligence for the intelligence for markets are growing over time

insights.claim: Micron fell about 20% in three days during the recent profit-taking and previously dropped roughly 30% in three weeks in March after blowout earnings, examples the hosts cite of sharp drawdowns the market quickly 'pretends never happened.'
insights.speaker: Michael Batnick
insights.so_what: Even leading AI/memory names carry 20-30% drawdown risk on no fundamental change; position-size for that volatility.
insights.quote: Micron fell 30% in like 3 weeks in March after a blowout earnings number. And we just like all of these declines, we just pretend they never happened.

insights.claim: Carlson argues Robinhood's new agentic AI trading (AI agents connected via an MCP server that research, trade and rebalance) will hurt users because they will constantly tinker with rules, defeating the discipline a back-test requires.
insights.speaker: Ben Carlson
insights.so_what: Agentic retail trading may amplify behavioral churn rather than improve outcomes; a risk for both users and market microstructure.
insights.quote: the whole point of a back test is in the back test, you don't go and change it every time it doesn't work. You stick with it no matter what.

themes.theme: Global broadening: EM earnings and diversification

insights.claim: Per a Schroders/idea-farm deck, 2026 equity gains are being driven by earnings rather than valuation, with EM equities up 26% year-to-date (through end of May) and EM earnings 'dwarfing' the US, Japan and others.
insights.speaker: Ben Carlson
insights.so_what: The AI trade is global and earnings-led; EM (via semis) is an under-owned way to participate in the same theme.
insights.quote: EM equities are up 26% year-to date... look at look at the earning for emerging markets. It's kind of crazy. It's just dwarfing everything else

insights.claim: The EM index composition has flipped: China fell from nearly 40% of the index in 2021 to roughly half that, while Korea and Taiwan (driven by names like SK Hynix and Samsung) are now individually larger than China.
insights.speaker: Ben Carlson
insights.so_what: EM is now effectively an AI-semiconductor bet via Korea/Taiwan rather than a China bet; index buyers get very different exposure than in 2021.
insights.quote: in 2021, not that long ago, China was almost 40% of the EM index... Korea and Taiwan are now bigger than China

insights.claim: Citing Fidelity's Jurrien Timmer (via Jason Zweig), the hosts note European equities are severely underweight tech outside ASML and could act as a 'port in the storm,' falling less if the S&P 500 drops because there is less price buildup to unwind.
insights.speaker: Ben Carlson
insights.so_what: European 'old economy' equities offer a way to cut exposure to the one big AI trade while staying invested.
insights.quote: European stocks may not shoot the lights out but if the S&P 500 goes down they will probably go down less because there's less price buildup that would get undone

themes.theme: Consumer resilience and the locked-in mortgage effect

insights.claim: Carlson highlights Capital One's CEO saying that, on data alone, the consumer looks strong with unemployment very strong — a signal he weights because it comes from a mass-market lender (not premium AmEx) and the stock is in a ~23% drawdown.
insights.speaker: Ben Carlson
insights.so_what: A mass-market lender's positive consumer read is a high-signal counter to recession fears; watch for the CEO turning cautious as the real tell.
insights.quote: I think the consumer is really the strong shoulders the economy stands on. Unemployment continues to be very strong.

insights.claim: Carlson argues consumer strength is anchored by housing: ~35% of household budgets is housing, and many homeowners locked in both low prices and sub-3% mortgages, effectively borrowing below the 4.2% inflation rate and giving themselves a recurring real 'raise.'
insights.speaker: Ben Carlson
insights.so_what: The 3% mortgage cohort is a durable, under-appreciated buffer for consumer spending; if those holders had to reprice to 5.5%, spending would drop sharply.
insights.quote: I'm borrowing for free for my house at 3%. on a real basis right now

insights.claim: Existing home sales accelerated to the fastest pace of the year in May at a 4.17 million annualized rate, with the median existing-home price up 1.3% year-over-year to $429,000 and at all-time highs.
insights.speaker: Michael Batnick
insights.so_what: Improving sales off all-time-high prices, despite rising mortgage rates, supports a tentative bull case on housing even if levels remain historically low.
insights.quote: contract closings rising to an annualized rate of 4.17 million... the median sales price of an existing home climbed 1.3% from a year ago to 429,000

insights.claim: Carlson is turning bullish on residential real estate stocks, reasoning that buyers are gradually accepting current prices as the new normal even though affordability remains poor and mortgage rates rose this year.
insights.speaker: Ben Carlson
insights.so_what: An early, hedged contrarian long on housing stocks on a 'this is just what houses cost now' acceptance thesis, supported by the largest young-buyer cohort needing homes.
insights.quote: I am starting to get um bullish on residential real estate stocks. It might be early. This might be this might age horribly.

insights.claim: Both hosts push back on AI 'doomer' job-loss takes, citing a WSJ survey of economists where most saw no net job change and respondents leaned roughly 2-to-1 toward AI complementing rather than replacing workers.
insights.speaker: Ben Carlson
insights.so_what: Economist consensus skews toward AI-as-complement, tempering near-term white-collar-collapse trades; the displacement risk is regional/industry-specific.
insights.quote: is AI a technology more likely to replace workers or complement them? Um, it was overwhelmingly compliment. It looks like it was about 2 to one.

insights.claim: Carlson predicts AI regulation will be unhelpful regardless of form, and argues AI labs invited it by hyping catastrophic risks (e.g. claims of destroying 50% of white-collar jobs or enabling bioweapons) while failing to self-regulate.
insights.speaker: Ben Carlson
insights.so_what: Regulatory risk for AI firms is rising and likely clumsy; the hype cycle that drove valuations also invites a policy overhang.
insights.quote: if you've been saying we're going to destroy 50% of white collar jobs... you don't get to complain about whatever the government does

insights.claim: The hosts reference a reported US government export-control directive (described as suspending all foreign-national access to 'Fable 5' and 'Mythos 5') as evidence AI companies are beginning to face national-security regulation.
insights.speaker: Ben Carlson
insights.so_what: Export controls on frontier models add a geopolitical/regulatory constraint on AI distribution that could affect the sector's growth runway.
insights.quote: issued an export control directive to suspend all access to Fable 5 and Mythos 5 by any foreign national whether inside or outside the United States
